questionText: Two places A and B are 80 km apart from each other on a highway. A car starts from A and another from B at the same time. If they move in the same direction they meet each other in 8 hours. If they move in opposite directions towards each other, they meet in 1 hour 20 minutes. Determine the speeds of the cars.

steps.explanation: Let the speeds of the two cars be $x$ kmph and $y$ kmph respectively (where $x > y$).
When moving in the same direction, the relative speed is $(x - y)$ kmph. They cover 80 km in 8 hours.
When moving in opposite directions, the relative speed is $(x + y)$ kmph. They meet in 1 hr 20 min $= \frac{4}{3}$ hr.

solution.shortcut: Use relative speed: same direction gives difference, opposite directions gives sum. Set up two equations and solve.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to convert 1 hour 20 minutes to $\frac{4}{3}$ hours before forming the equation.

questionText: A man takes 6 hours 30 min in going by a cycle and coming back by scooter. He would have lost 2 hours 10 min by going on cycle both ways. How long would it take him to go by scooter both ways?

steps.explanation: Let the one-way distance be $x$ km. The time for one-way cycle + one-way scooter = 6 hr 30 min.
Doubling both sides: time for two-way cycle + time for two-way scooter = 13 hours.

Going by cycle both ways takes 2 hr 10 min more than going cycle one-way and scooter one-way: $T_{cycle}(2x) = 6\ \text{hr}\ 30\ \text{min} + 2\ \text{hr}\ 10\ \text{min} = 8\ \text{hr}\ 40\ \text{min}$.
Subtract the cycle two-way time from 13 hours to get the scooter two-way time.

solution.shortcut: Double the one-way mixed trip time to get total two-way time (cycle + scooter). Then subtract the given two-way cycle time to get the two-way scooter time.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing one-way time with two-way time when applying the 2 hr 10 min penalty.

questionText: Sneha is picked up by her father by car from college everyday. The college gets over at 4 p.m. daily. One day, the college got over an hour earlier than usual. Sneha started walking towards her house. Her father, unaware of this fact, leaves his house as usual, meets his daughter on the way, picks her up and they reach the house 15 minutes earlier than usual. What is the ratio of the father's driving speed to Sneha's walking speed?

steps.explanation: Since they arrive 15 minutes early, the father saved 15 minutes of driving time total. This means he drove 7.5 minutes less in each direction (to and from the meeting point instead of going all the way to college).
steps.result: Father drove 7.5 min less than usual in each direction

steps.explanation: The father normally reaches college at 4 p.m. Since he met Sneha 7.5 min before reaching the college-end of his route, the meeting happened at $4:00 - 7.5\ \text{min} = 3:52:30\ \text{p.m.}$ i.e., $3:52\frac{1}{2}$ p.m.
Sneha started walking at 3:00 p.m. (college got over 1 hour early). She walked until 3:52.5 p.m., so she walked for 52.5 minutes.
In that same duration, Sneha's father covered the same distance in 7.5 minutes (by car). Since the same distance is covered, speed is inversely proportional to time.

solution.shortcut: Time saved (15 min) = 2 × (time father would have driven from meeting point to college). So father would have taken 7.5 min for that extra stretch. Sneha walked that same distance in 52.5 min (from 3:00 to 3:52:30). Ratio = 52.5 / 7.5 = 7.
solution.commonMistake: Thinking the 15 minutes saved applies only in one direction instead of being split across both directions of the detour avoided.

formulas.title: Speed inversely proportional to time (same distance)
